name: كَانَ + صِفَة — Past States with كَانَ

cards.grammarNote: كَانَ + adjective describes a past state. The adjective is in the accusative case (tanwin -an): سَعِيدًا, not سَعِيدٌ. This is because كَانَ is a verb that governs an accusative predicate (خَبَر كَانَ).
The predicate adjective agrees with the subject in gender: كَانَتْ (she was, f.) + حَزِينَةً (sad, f. accusative). The feminine accusative indefinite ends in ـَةً (-atan).
كَانُوا (kānū) = they were (masculine/mixed plural). The plural accusative adjective مُتْعَبِينَ ends in ـِينَ (-īna), the sound masculine plural accusative/genitive ending.
كُنْتُ (kuntu) = I was. The 1st person singular of كَانَ. The adjective خَائِفًا (afraid, m.) is in the accusative. A female speaker would say: كُنْتُ خَائِفَةً (kuntu khāʾifatan).
كُنَّا (kunnā) = we were. The plural predicate adjective مُتَحَمِّسِينَ takes the masculine plural accusative form. If a group of women: كُنَّا مُتَحَمِّسَاتٍ (sound feminine plural).
Negation of كَانَ + adjective: كَانَ غَيْرَ + adjective (lit. 'was other-than'). Or: لَمْ يَكُنْ مُرْتَاحًا (lam yakun murtāḥan) = he was not comfortable, using لَمْ + jussive of كَانَ.

سُعَدَاءَ (suʿadāʾa) is the broken plural of سَعِيد. Many Arabic adjectives form their plurals through internal vowel changes (broken plural) rather than adding a suffix. سُعَدَاءَ is accusative (no tanwin for words ending in ء preceded by alif).
